preFix.title: Decorating a scrapbook album cover
preFix.subtitle: Transforming a plain bought album into a personalised piece
preFix.excerpt: A plain cloth or leatherette album cover becomes a personalised object with a few layers of mixed media: a gesso primer, colour washes, washi tape geometry, or tissue-paper collage. This tutorial covers three design approaches, each built from the same priming and sealing steps.
preFix.sourceNotes: Scrapbook album decoration using mixed-media techniques is a contemporary craft with no pre-1928 documented source. The gesso-priming and collage methods draw on general artist-preparation and decoupage technique documented across 20th-century craft literature.

content.text: A bought album comes with a generic cloth or leatherette cover that carries no particular personality. One evening of straightforward work changes that. The techniques here are not precious; none of them require experience with painting or printmaking. The first coat of gesso is the most important step; after that, the cover accepts paint, tape, and paper collage without any special preparation.

items.qty: Small tub; a few tablespoons is enough for a single album
items.name: White gesso
Palette knife (small, flexible)
items.substitutions: Optional; useful for applying gesso to texture paste effects. A stiff brush also works.

items.qty: 2–4 rolls in complementary patterns or colours
items.name: Washi tape
Coloured tissue paper
items.substitutions: Optional; for the tissue-collage approach only.

content.text: Priming the cover
The first step for all three design approaches is the same. Mix a small amount of gesso with water in roughly a 2:1 ratio (two parts gesso to one part water). Brush one coat of this diluted
gesso primer

over the front cover using the paste brush. Work in long strokes from top to bottom. Let it dry completely; at least twenty minutes; longer if the room is cool. The gesso dries to a matte, slightly toothy surface that accepts paint and adhesives.

A cloth cover absorbs the gesso readily. A leatherette cover may resist it slightly; press firmly with the brush to work the gesso into the texture. One coat is enough for watercolour and washi-tape work; two thin coats give a better ground for tissue-paper collage.
Design approach 1: single colour wash with title
Load the paste brush with a diluted watercolour mixed on a spare plate or the palette lid. The colour should be noticeably transparent against white paper; this is a
watercolour wash
, not a full coat of opaque paint. Brush it across the primed cover in long, slightly uneven strokes. The white gesso shows through the wash and the brush marks add texture. Allow to dry.

Add a title or date to the spine or the front cover in a darker tone of the same colour, or in black. A fine brush and undiluted watercolour gives control over letterforms. Press the cover under a flat weight for thirty minutes after the lettering dries.
Design approach 2: geometric washi-tape pattern

Plan the tape layout on a rough sketch before applying to the cover; tape lines are difficult to reposition cleanly once pressed down. Horizontal strips across the cover at even spacing are the simplest pattern. Diagonal strips create more movement. A combined grid of horizontal and vertical strips makes a plaid. Two or three tape widths and patterns give more interest than a single roll.

Press each strip along its length with a finger, smoothing from centre to edges. Trim the overhanging ends at the cover edge with the craft knife against a ruler. Gesso-primed cloth takes washi tape adhesive well and holds it flat without buckling.
Design approach 3: layered tissue-paper collage
Tear (do not cut) the tissue paper into irregular pieces roughly 4-8 cm across. Tearing gives soft organic edges that blend into each other; cut edges are obvious and harder to blend.

Dilute PVA to a 1:1 ratio with water. Brush a thin coat of diluted PVA onto one small area of the primed cover. Lay a torn tissue piece over the wet PVA, then brush another thin coat of diluted PVA over the top of the tissue. Smooth out any large bubbles or wrinkles with the brush; small wrinkles add texture and are part of the effect. Overlap pieces to cover the full cover surface. Work in sections: wet, apply, coat over.

Allow to dry fully (at least thirty minutes with the cover open so both faces can breathe). Add a second layer of torn tissue in a second colour if more depth is wanted. Finish with one coat of diluted PVA over everything as a
decoupage seal
. This coat also adds a slight sheen.
Finishing

Leave the finished cover open while it dries to prevent the boards from warping. Press under a flat weight overnight once fully dry. Label the spine with the album title or date using a paint pen or a fine brush and acrylic paint; this makes the album findable on a shelf.
Care

A gesso-and-PVA-sealed cover is water-resistant but not waterproof. Keep albums away from damp storage and direct sunlight. The sealing coat can be refreshed with another thin layer of diluted PVA if the surface begins to feel rough or the tissue lifts at an edge.

initialFindings.message: PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
